title: Decide on Your Vending Entity
quote: If you fail to plan, you are planning to fail.
description: Before you buy machines or stock snacks, you must establish a solid legal and financial foundation. Operating as an individual without a formal business structure exposes your personal assets to liability if someone gets hurt or a location sues.

tasks.text: Choose your vending business name.
Research LLC/entity requirements in your state.
Apply for an EIN through the IRS if eligible.
Open a business bank account.
Create a simple business email, website, or social profile.

tips: Keep personal and business finances strictly separate to maintain your LLC liability protection.
An EIN is free from the IRS website. Avoid third-party services that charge fees to file it for you.

title: Select Your Beginner Vending Model
description: Vending machines are not all the same. Your equipment choice dictates your product cost, location options, maintenance needs, and growth strategy. Choose the model that fits your budget and time.

tasks.text: Pick your beginner vending model (Bulk, Snack, Cold Drink, or Combo).
Review budget constraints (Bulk: under $500, Combo: $1,500-$4,000).
Decide if you will start with cash-only or plan for digital upgrades.

tips: Bulk Vending (gumballs, toys) has the lowest barrier to entry and works well in local pizzerias, laundromats, and barber shops.
Snack/Drink Combos are highly popular but require reliable refrigeration and MDB compatibility if you want card readers.

title: Brainstorm and Map Out 25 Potential Locations
description: Vending is entirely about placement. A machine in a high-traffic location will make money even with poor product selection, but a machine in a dead location will fail even with premium stock. Start by mapping out opportunities within 20 minutes of your home.

tasks.text: Write down 25 possible locations near your home.
Categorize them (e.g., offices, warehouses, laundromats, auto shops).
Identify the decision-maker or manager for each prospect.

tips: Look for places where people wait: auto repair waiting rooms, laundromats, or warehouse break rooms.
Keep your route tight. A machine 45 minutes away eats up your profit in gas and travel time.

title: Execute the Outreach and Pitch
description: Getting locations requires active outreach. You are offering a free amenity to improve their employee or customer experience. Use Snaxology's proven script to open doors and overcome common objections.

tasks.text: Contact at least 10 locations this week.
Use the pitch script: 'We install, stock, and service the machine at zero cost to you.'
Overcome the commission objection by focusing on service first.

tips: Focus on service: 'We keep it clean, stocked, and working. If you want a commission, we can discuss that once we see the volume, but our main value is reliable service.'
Always leave a professional flyer or business card with your Snaxology-branded contact details.

title: Secure the Placement Agreement
description: Before you buy or move any machine, secure the agreement in writing. A simple, clear agreement protects your placement, outlines responsibilities, and prevents sudden removals.

tasks.text: Draft a simple 1-page placement agreement.
Specify who pays for electricity (usually the location).
Define the exit clause (e.g., 30 days notice for either party).
Confirm the exact physical spot for the machine.

tips: Keep it simple. A 5-page legal document scares small business owners. A 1-page 'Service Agreement' feels cooperative.
Make sure the outlet is reliable and not connected to a light switch that gets turned off at night.

title: Inspect and Purchase the Right Equipment
description: Never buy a machine sight unseen. Used equipment can have hidden electrical, coin mechanism, or refrigeration issues that cost hundreds of dollars to fix. Take a physical checklist and test everything before paying.

tasks.text: Locate a machine on Facebook Marketplace, Craigslist, or local distributors.
Inspect the machine using the physical inspection checklist.
Verify MDB compatibility if you plan to add cashless payments.
Negotiate the price based on any defects found.

tips: Bring rolls of quarters, dimes, nickels, and dollar bills to test the bill validator and coin mech multiple times.
If a drink machine is warm, walk away unless you are a refrigeration technician or getting it practically free.

title: Safe Delivery, Installation, and Stocking
description: Moving a 600-pound machine is dangerous. Plan the logistics carefully, measure every doorway, and stock the machine with proven crowd-pleasers rather than niche snacks.

tasks.text: Measure doorways, hallways, and elevators at the location.
Secure an appliance dolly, heavy-duty straps, and helper support.
Purchase inventory from wholesale clubs (Costco, Sam's Club, BJ's).
Stock the machine: 80% proven sellers, 20% test items.

tips: Proven sellers: Coca-Cola, Pepsi, Sprite, Doritos, Lay's, Snickers, M&Ms, and bottled water.
Always unplug the machine before moving it and let it sit upright for a few hours before plugging it in so the compressor oil settles.

title: Track Cash Flow, Profit, and Reinvestment
description: Revenue is not profit. To grow, you must track every machine separately, understand your gross margins, and reinvest your early profits back into the business.

tasks.text: Set up a separate spreadsheet or ledger for each machine.
Track Gross Sales, Cost of Goods Sold (COGS), and Operating Expenses.
Calculate your machine's Payback Period.
Reinvest 100% of early profits into inventory, card readers, or the next machine.

tips: A healthy gross margin on snacks and drinks should be 50% to 60%. If you buy a soda for $0.50, sell it for $1.25 to $1.50.
Do not spend your profits on personal items until your route is fully self-sustaining.

title: Go Digital with Cashless Upgrades
description: Adding a card reader can increase sales by 30% to 40% because fewer people carry cash. But digital upgrades come with equipment costs and transaction fees. Add them when your volume justifies the cost.

tasks.text: Evaluate if the location volume justifies a card reader ($10-$15/mo fee + 3-5% transaction fee).
Order a compatible card reader (e.g., Nayax, Cantaloupe).
Install and test mobile pay, credit card, and remote telemetry.

tips: Cashless readers are ideal for offices, universities, and hospitals, but cash is still king in heavy industrial or construction sites.
Telemetry allows you to see what has sold from your phone, saving you unnecessary trips.

title: Execute Growth and Route Expansion Plays
description: Once your first machine is running smoothly, you can run advanced growth plays. Buy existing routes, upgrade equipment, or offer premium micro-markets and smart coolers to high-value locations.

tasks.text: Analyze underperforming machines and prepare to relocate them if necessary.
Look for existing-route purchase opportunities and verify their sales numbers.
Introduce premium smart coolers or micro-markets for high-end clients.

tips: If a machine makes less than $100/mo after 90 days, move it. Don't fall in love with a bad location.
Snaxology's premium AI smart coolers and micro-markets are excellent upsells for offices with 100+ employees.
